title: Granite marbling pattern
subtitle: Dropped pigment marbling without combing for a stone-like surface
excerpt: Granite marbling is the simplest carrageenan-bath pattern and the best starting point for anyone new to Western marbling. Colours are dropped onto the size and allowed to spread naturally without combing or stylus work. The result is a mottled, stone-like pattern with irregular spots of contrasting colour.
sourceNotes: Joseph Halfer, The Progress of the Marbling Art (translated from the German, first published 1885, public domain via Internet Archive), on stone and spot marbling as the foundational pattern in carrageenan-bath Western marbling.

glossaryTerms.definition: A thickened water solution made from carrageenan (seaweed extract) that holds pigments on the surface of the marbling tray without mixing. The viscosity of the size determines how far each dropped colour spreads.

A natural surfactant added drop-by-drop to marbling pigments to reduce their surface tension, controlling how far each colour spreads when dropped onto the size. Too much ox-gall pushes all other colours to the edges; too little produces tight blobs that don't spread.
Pre-treating paper with a potash alum solution so that the marbling pigments adhere when the paper is laid on the size. Unmordanted paper lifts no colour and the pattern does not transfer.

content.text: Granite marbling depends entirely on balance: the balance of the
in each colour, and the balance of colours dropped in sequence. Get the chemistry right and the pattern forms itself.

attrs.heading: Prepare the carrageenan size the night before
attrs.body: Mix 2 teaspoons of carrageenan powder with 2 litres of cold water. Blend for 2 minutes. Leave overnight in the tray. The size settles and degasses over 12 hours, which prevents bubbles in the finished pattern.

items.name: Marbling tray
items.notes: At least A4 size and at least 50 mm deep. A clean food-storage tub works.

items.name: Carrageenan (marbling size)
items.notes: Prepared the night before. Should be the consistency of thin cream.

items.name: Liquid acrylic marbling colours, 3 or 4
items.notes: Choose colours that contrast. A dark base with two mid-tones and a light accent reads as classic granite.

items.name: Ox-gall
items.notes: Add one drop to each cup of pigment. Test spread on the size before committing to the full tray.

items.name: Eyedropper
items.notes: One per colour. Glass droppers give more consistent drop sizes.

items.name: Alum-mordanted paper
items.notes: Brush a solution of 2 tablespoons of potash alum in 500 ml of warm water onto the paper and allow to dry completely before marbling.

content.text: solution onto both sides of the paper and allow to dry completely. Paper mordanted on one side only must be laid mordant-side down on the size. Mark the mordanted side lightly with a pencil in the corner.

Drop the darkest colour first from about 10 cm above the size: it spreads to form a ground layer. Wait five seconds, then drop the second colour into the centres of the first spots: it pushes the first outward, forming rings. Continue with subsequent colours in a scattered, irregular pattern. For a granite effect, drop randomly rather than in rows. The pattern is complete when the surface is filled.

Hold the mordanted paper by two corners and lower it gently, centre first, onto the size. Avoid trapping air bubbles by lowering at an angle. Leave for 30 seconds, then lift straight up from one corner in a single smooth pull. Rinse the paper immediately under a slow stream of cold water to remove the carrageenan size from the surface. Dry flat.

items.symptom: Colours sink through the size and pool at the bottom
items.cause: The size is too thin or the pigments are too heavy.
items.fix: Increase the carrageenan concentration slightly (add another half-teaspoon per litre). Add an extra drop of ox-gall to the pigment to increase spread.

items.symptom: No pattern transfers to the paper
items.cause: The paper was not mordanted with alum, or the alum was not dry before marbling.
items.fix: Re-mordant the paper and allow to dry fully. The alum must be completely dry before contact with the size.
